...
\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}

The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
linear algebra), we package the construction here.

\begin{definition}\leanok
[Grassmannian scheme]
  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
  % + L773-L850).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
  %  the Grassmannian scheme
  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
  %  contravariant functor
  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
  %  set of all equivalence classes
  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)

python3 - <<'EOF'
p = 'blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex'
s = open(p).read()

anchor = """\\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
\\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}"""
assert anchor in s

nodes = r"""\subsection{Base-change well-definedness of the Quot functor}
\label{sec:quot_base_change}

The pullback action of the Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) along an
\(S\)-morphism \(T' \to T\) is well defined because each of the four
conditions on a family of quotients is preserved by base change; this is the
content of the following four lemmas.  Throughout, write
\(g' : X_{T'} \to X_T\) for the induced morphism on relative products, which
is cartesian over \(T' \to T\).

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[Pullback preserves finite presentation]
  \label{lem:pullback_finite_presentation}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isFinitePresentation}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1 (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L418-L419):
  % "Then as properness and flatness are preserved by base-change, and as
  %  tensor-product is right exact, the pull-back of <F, q> ... is
  %  well-defined"; cf. Stacks, Modules, "pullback of finitely presented
  %  modules is finitely presented".
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(g : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) a finitely
  presented sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (locally the cokernel of a map
  of finite free modules).  Then \(g^* \mathcal{F}\) is a finitely presented
  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  % NOTE: the Lean proof is complete modulo the index-bookkeeping leaf
  % `Modules.pullbackSlicePresentation_isFinite` (the transported presentation
  % keeps its index types; the definitional reduction certifying this is
  % recorded as a typed sorry for performance reasons).
  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on whose members \(\mathcal{F}\)
  admits presentations
  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus b_i} \to \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus a_i} \to
  \mathcal{F}|_{U_i} \to 0\) with \(a_i, b_i\) finite.  The preimages
  \(g^{-1}(U_i)\) cover \(Y\).  The pullback functor is a left adjoint, hence
  right exact, and it sends \(\mathcal{O}_X\) to \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) and finite
  free modules to finite free modules of the same rank; moreover restriction
  to \(g^{-1}(U_i)\) of the pullback is the pullback of the restriction along
  \(g^{-1}(U_i) \to U_i\).  Applying the restricted pullback to the chosen
  presentation therefore yields a presentation
  \(\mathcal{O}^{\oplus b_i} \to \mathcal{O}^{\oplus a_i} \to
  (g^*\mathcal{F})|_{g^{-1}(U_i)} \to 0\) with the same finite index sets.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[Flatness over the base is stable under base change]
  \label{lem:coherent_flat_base_change}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat.of_isPullback}
  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1 (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L418-L419);
  % cf. Stacks 01U9 (flatness of morphisms is stable under base change).
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Stacks 01U9.}
  Let
  \[
    \begin{array}{ccc}
      X' & \xrightarrow{\ g'\ } & X \\
      \downarrow f' & & \downarrow f \\
      S' & \xrightarrow{\ g\ } & S
    \end{array}
  \]
  be a cartesian square of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf
  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is flat over \(S\) in the sense of
  \cref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}.  Then \(g'^* \mathcal{F}\) is flat over
  \(S'\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  For a quasi-coherent module, the affine-pair flatness predicate of
  \cref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat} is equivalent to flatness of every stalk
  \(\mathcal{F}_x\) over the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S, f(x)}\): a module
  over a ring \(B\) with an action of \(A\) through \(A \to B\) is flat over
  \(A\) if and only if all its localizations at primes of \(B\) are flat over
  the corresponding localizations of \(A\).  The stalk of \(g'^*\mathcal{F}\)
  at \(x' \in X'\) is
  \(\mathcal{F}_{g'(x')} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, g'(x')}}
  \mathcal{O}_{X', x'}\), and since the square is cartesian
  \(\mathcal{O}_{X', x'}\) is a localization of
  \(\mathcal{O}_{X, g'(x')} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{S, s}}
  \mathcal{O}_{S', s'}\) (where \(s = f(g'(x'))\), \(s' = f'(x')\)).  Hence
  \((g'^*\mathcal{F})_{x'}\) is a localization of
  \(\mathcal{F}_{g'(x')} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{S,s}} \mathcal{O}_{S', s'}\);
  flatness over \(\mathcal{O}_{S,s}\) base-changes to flatness over
  \(\mathcal{O}_{S', s'}\) and is preserved by localization.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[Proper support is stable under base change]
  \label{lem:proper_support_base_change}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport.of_isPullback}
  \uses{def:has_proper_support, def:modules_annihilator}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1 (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L418-L419);
  % cf. Stacks 01W4 (properness is stable under base change) and 056H
  % (support of the pullback of a finite-type module).
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  In the cartesian square of \cref{lem:coherent_flat_base_change}, let
  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a finitely presented sheaf of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose schematic support is proper over \(S\)
  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}).  Then the schematic support of
  \(g'^* \mathcal{F}\) is proper over \(S'\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  A local section of \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) pulls back to a section
  annihilating \(g'^* \mathcal{F}\), so the pullback ideal
  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F}) \cdot \mathcal{O}_{X'}\) is contained in
  \(\mathrm{Ann}(g'^*\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}).
  Consequently the schematic support of \(g'^* \mathcal{F}\) is a closed
  subscheme of the base change
  \(Z' := Z \times_X X' = Z \times_S S'\) of the schematic support
  \(Z\) of \(\mathcal{F}\).  Since \(Z \to S\) is proper and properness is
  stable under base change, \(Z' \to S'\) is proper; a closed immersion
  followed by a proper morphism is proper, so the schematic support of
  \(g'^* \mathcal{F}\) is proper over \(S'\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[The fibrewise Hilbert function is invariant under base change]
  \label{lem:hilbert_fibre_base_change}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertFunction_quotBaseMap}
  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
  % Polynomials" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L498):
  % the decomposition Quot = \coprod_\Phi Quot^\Phi presupposes that the
  % Hilbert polynomial of the fibres is invariant under base change.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(\pi : X \to S\) be locally of finite type, \(L\) a sheaf of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, \(T' \to T\) a morphism of \(S\)-schemes, and
  \(\mathcal{F}\) a finitely presented sheaf on \(X_T\) whose schematic
  support is proper over \(T\).  For \(t' \in T'\) with image \(t \in T\)
  and every \(m \in \mathbb{N}\),
  \[
    \dim_{\kappa(t')} \Gamma\bigl((X_{T'})_{t'},\,
      (g'^*\mathcal{F})_{t'} \otimes L_{t'}^{\otimes m}\bigr)
    \;=\;
    \dim_{\kappa(t)} \Gamma\bigl((X_T)_t,\,
      \mathcal{F}_t \otimes L_t^{\otimes m}\bigr),
  \]
  where \(g' : X_{T'} \to X_T\) is the induced morphism.  In particular the
  graded Hilbert function of \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} is invariant under
  base change of the parameter scheme.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  Since \(X_{T'} = X_T \times_T T'\), the fibre of \(X_{T'} \to T'\) at
  \(t'\) is the base change \((X_T)_t \times_{\operatorname{Spec} \kappa(t)}
  \operatorname{Spec} \kappa(t')\) of the fibre at \(t\) along the residue
  field extension \(\kappa(t) \to \kappa(t')\), and under this identification
  \((g'^*\mathcal{F})_{t'} \otimes L_{t'}^{\otimes m}\) is the pullback of
  \(\mathcal{F}_t \otimes L_t^{\otimes m}\).  Sections of a module
  annihilated by the ideal of a closed subscheme are computed on that
  subscheme, so both sides are computed on the support of the twisted
  module, which is proper — in particular quasi-compact and separated — over
  the respective residue field.  Global sections of a quasi-coherent module
  on a qcqs scheme commute with flat base change of the (affine) base; a
  field extension \(\kappa(t) \to \kappa(t')\) is flat, so
  \(\Gamma\) of the base-changed module is
  \(\Gamma\bigl((X_T)_t, \mathcal{F}_t \otimes L_t^{\otimes m}\bigr)
  \otimes_{\kappa(t)} \kappa(t')\).  Finally
  \(\dim_{\kappa(t')} (V \otimes_{\kappa(t)} \kappa(t')) =
  \dim_{\kappa(t)} V\) for any \(\kappa(t)\)-vector space \(V\) (in the
  infinite-dimensional case both dimensions are infinite, and both sides of
  the displayed equality carry the same junk value).
\end{proof}

\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}"""

s = s.replace(anchor, nodes)
open(p,'w').write(s)
print("part 2 done")
EOF

Universe forced `Type u → Type (u+1)` (same as picSharp/merged-Grassmannian precedent). Old stubs deleted from `QuotScheme.lean` (now 3 sorries = FBC leaves only).

**The functor-law recipe (reusable for any Setoid-quotient functor of module pullbacks):**
- Prove ONCE at app level: `Modules.pullback_id_app_coherence` ((†): congr∘Comp∘congr∘pullbackId collapse = 𝟙, from `pseudofunctor_right_unitality`) and `Modules.pullback_comp_app_coherence(_inv)` ((◇′): from `pseudofunctor_associativity`). Both by `subst` on the equalities (state them with FREE rhs variables so subst works!) + one simp pass unpacking whisker apps + congrArg-composition with `Iso.hom_inv_id_app(_assoc)` to move invertible prefixes + **final `exact lawV` closing by DEFEQ despite leftover 𝟙/eqToHom padding** — comp-with-𝟙 in `X.Modules` is defeq; do NOT try to simp the padding away (instance-transparency walls block `Category.comp_id/id_comp` in `(F ⋙ G).obj`/`𝟭.obj`-typed slots).
- `Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr = eqToIso`; component lemmas `pullbackCongr_hom_app/_inv_app h M = eqToHom _` by `subst h; simp [pullbackCongr]`.
- map_id/map_comp squares: Rel-iso := `pullbackCongr (quotBaseMap_id/comp) .app F ≪≫ pullbackId/pullbackComp.symm .app F`; then `change` to explicit form, `rw [assoc, congr.hom.naturality_assoc q, pullbackId/Comp.inv.naturality q]`, `simp only [pullbackTriangleIso, Iso.trans_inv, Iso.app_inv, assoc, Functor.map_comp]`, `rw [reassoc_of% key]`, `simp only [Functor.map_comp]; rfl`.
- Gotchas: functor into `Type (u+1)` needs `TypeCat.ofHom` around `Quotient.map`; `(𝟙 T.unop).left`/`(f ≫ g).left` need explicit `Over.Hom.left (...)` inside `change` (dot-notation fails on non-variable heads under heartbeat pressure); `Over.comp_left` takes explicit `_ _ _ f g`; `pullback.map` is an abbrev over `lift` — pass `pullback.lift_fst/snd(+_assoc)` to simp explicitly; both functor defs need `set_option maxHeartbeats 1600000` + `backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false`; structure-field `flat := fun {U} hU {V} hV e => lemma ... hU hV e` (manual eta) dodges a ∀-Prop elaboration eta-bug.

data.content:
---
name: horizon-inbox
description: Read and act on the Archon Horizon inbox — list/filter agent-ready items, understand labels/kinds, comment, close, create, and message other projects via the `horizon inbox` CLI.
---

The inbox is the durable channel between the human, the Ground agent, the
Horizon agent, and other projects. Act on it through the CLI; never edit inbox
files by hand. Prefer `--json` whenever reading command output for exact ids or
fields.

## Read

- `horizon inbox list` — read the inbox. Use `--json` when you need exact ids,
  labels, scope, author, audience, timestamps, or comments. Use the list filters
  to narrow without losing the overview: `--to horizon`, `--to ground`,
  `--to human`, `--project P`, repeated `--kind K`, repeated `--label L`,
  `--status open`, `--query TEXT`, `--limit N`, and `--comments N`.
- Labels are the release gate. Local inbox items default to `agent-ready`, which
  means they are visible to agents at run boundaries. Keep that default for
  machine-addressed work, and usually keep it for human-addressed notices too:
  it helps later agents see that the human has already been notified and avoids
  duplicate reports. Use `not-ready`, `rejected`, or no labels only when there is
  a concrete reason agents should not see or act on the item yet.
- Audience says who should read the item: empty/general, `horizon`, `ground`,
  `human`, or `project:<name>`. Scope/project says what the item is about.
- Horizon reads general items, items addressed to `horizon`, and items addressed
  to its project. It should ignore other projects' addressed items.
- Kinds: `hint` (normal guidance), `issue` (problem to fix/report),
  `protection` (standing constraint / soft freeze), `info` (a notice for the
  human — something you did or noticed they should know, e.g. a renamed project
  or an important change; purely informational, never affects what runs),
  `memory` (durable agent note or dead end).

## Act

Authorship is automatic: every item and comment you create through the CLI is
attributed to your role (`ground` or `horizon`) from the run environment — you do
**not** pass `--author` (a stray `--author blueprint-reviewer` is demoted to the
`agent` metadata, not used as the author, so the author set stays conventional).
If you are a dispatched **subagent**, add `--agent <your-name>` (e.g. `--agent
blueprint-reviewer`) to record your identity; it is stored in metadata and shown
next to the role in the UI, keeping the author itself a clean role. Items and
comments are also auto-tagged with the run/session provenance (a small chip in the
UI); you do not pass this yourself.

New inbox items use a two-part body:

- First paragraph = concise title. Keep it short enough to scan in one UI row
  (aim for under ~100 characters); do not put the full explanation here.
- Second paragraph and later = non-empty description. Put the evidence, impact,
  next action, and relevant ids/files here. The description is **mandatory** — an
  item that is only a title is rejected.

Write bodies and comments in **Markdown**: the dashboard renders it. Use short
paragraphs and `-` bullet lists instead of one wall-of-text paragraph, `**bold**`
for the key claim, and backticks for ids, lemma names, files, and code
(`` `lem:foo` ``, `` `Foo.lean:42` ``). A long unstructured blob is hard to scan.

Do not create an item whose body is only a title. If you need a `[persistent]` or
`[temporary]` tag, put it at the start of the title paragraph and keep the rest

data.content:
`[temporary]` tag, put it at the start of the title paragraph and keep the rest
of that paragraph concise.

Before opening a new item, search the open inbox for the same topic using
`horizon inbox list --status open --query "key words" --json` and, when relevant,
`--project P` / `--to R`. If a live item already covers the same issue, do not
open a duplicate: add a comment with the new evidence, or edit the body if the
main description is stale. If you discover an open item is obsolete, already
solved, or superseded, add a short closing comment explaining why and then run
`horizon inbox complete <id>`.

- `horizon inbox comment <id> --body "..."` — progress note on an item (record
  what you did; not to ask the human for clarification). Do not write your name
  into the comment body; the author is set automatically. Drop a comment at each **key advance**
  while you work an item (a milestone reached, a route ruled out, a blocker hit),
  not only at the end — these comments are the item's visible progress trail.
- **Write comments as mathematics, for a human.** State the key step that
  succeeded in human-readable mathematical language (LaTeX for formulas), naming
  the result and the idea — e.g. "Proved $H^i(X,\mathcal{F})=0$ for $i>\dim X$ by
  reducing to the affine case via Čech cohomology." Mention Lean declaration
  names and file paths only as supporting detail; the substance is the maths, not
  a code changelog. This applies to comments on **roadmap items and tasks** too:
  when a key step lands, record it there in the same mathematical, legible style.
- `horizon inbox complete <id>` — close an item once you have acted on it. Before
  closing, add a short **closing comment**: first the mathematical conclusion (what
  was proved/established, in words + LaTeX), then a line of concise metadata
  (runs, approximate LOC, files/declarations involved). A reader should grasp the
  result and how it was concluded without opening the logs.
- `horizon inbox archive <id>` — soft-delete: keep the item for the record but
  hide it from the dashboard by default. Use for stale/superseded items that are
  not worth a closing comment; prefer `complete` (with a comment) for work you
  actually concluded. Ground is responsible for keeping the inbox tidy this way.
- `horizon inbox add --body "Short title\n\nDescription with details and next action." [--to R] [--project P] [--persistent|--temporary] [--agent <name> if a subagent]`
  — open a new item.
  - `--to` is the recipient: `horizon`, `ground`, `human`, or `project:<name>`.
    Use `--to project:<name>` to message another project (e.g. "I rewrote your
    `foo` declaration more cleanly, you may want it").
  - `--project` says what the item is ABOUT (its subject).
  - `--persistent` / `--temporary` just prepend a `[persistent]` / `[temporary]`
    tag to the body.
- `horizon inbox add --kind memory --to horizon --body "Short memory title\n\nDurable fact, convention, or dead end."` — record a durable

3653	  functor's pullback action requires.
3654	\end{definition}
3655	
3656	\begin{definition}
3657	
3658	\leanok
3659	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
3660	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
3661	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
3662	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises (2)" and
3663	  % "Grassmannian of a vector bundle" (read from
3664	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L562-L564
3665	  % + L584-L585).
3666	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3667	  % "$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
3668	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
3669	  %  [...]
3670	  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients
3671	  %  $q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,"
3672	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3673	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
3674	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
3675	  \(d\)} when there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) together with
3676	  isomorphisms \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus d}\) for
3677	  each \(i\).
3678	
3679	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not provide this predicate.
3680	\end{definition}
3681	
3682	\section{The Quot functor}
3683	\label{sec:quot_functor}
3684	
3685	\begin{definition}\leanok
3686	[Quot functor]
3687	  \label{def:quot_functor}
3688	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
3689	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support, def:coherent_sheaf_flat,
3690	    lem:pullback_finite_presentation, lem:coherent_flat_base_change,
3691	    lem:proper_support_base_change, lem:hilbert_fibre_base_change}
3692	  % NOTE (run 0010 T12): the Lean definition is now REAL
3693	  % (AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotFunctorDef.lean): value at T = Setoid quotient of
3694	  % `Scheme.QuotFamily` (finitely presented + flat + proper support + eventual-match
3695	  % Hilbert condition), morphism action = pullback along `quotBaseMap`.  The
3696	  % "coherent" clause is encoded as finite presentation
3697	  % (`SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation`), which over a locally noetherian base is
3698	  % coherence; condition (3) is encoded by eventual agreement of the graded Hilbert
3699	  % function with \(\Phi\) (exactly "the Hilbert polynomial of the fibre is
3700	  % \(\Phi\)" per def:hilbert_polynomial, excluding the junk-value coincidence).
3701	  % The functor is Type-((u+1))-valued (sheaves of modules are large); the
3702	  % representability statements are unaffected (RepresentableBy is
3703	  % universe-polymorphic).
3704	
3705	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
3706	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
3707	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
3708	  % + L481-L498).
3709	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3710	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
3711	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
3712	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
3713	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
3714	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
3715	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
3716	  %
3717	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
3718	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
3719	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
3720	  %
3721	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
3722	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
3723	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
3724	  %
3725	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
3726	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
3727	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
3728	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
3729	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
3730	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
3731	  %  well-defined, which gives
3732	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
3733	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
3734	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
3735	  %  [...]
3736	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
3737	  %  as a co-product
3738	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
3739	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
3740	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
3741	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
3742	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
3743	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
3744	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
3745	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3746	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
3747	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
3748	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
3749	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
3750	  \[
3751	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
3752	  \]
3753	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
3754	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
3755	  \begin{enumerate}
3756	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
3757	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
3758	      which is flat over \(T\);
3759	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
3760	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
3761	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
3762	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
3763	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
3764	      \(\Phi\).
3765	  \end{enumerate}
3766	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
3767	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
3768	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
3769	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
3770	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
3771	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
3772	\end{definition}
3773	
3774	\subsection{Base-change well-definedness of the Quot functor}
3775	\label{sec:quot_base_change}
3776	
3777	The pullback action of the Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) along an
3778	\(S\)-morphism \(T' \to T\) is well defined because each of the four
3779	conditions on a family of quotients is preserved by base change; this is the
3780	content of the following four lemmas.  Throughout, write
3781	\(g' : X_{T'} \to X_T\) for the induced morphism on relative products, which
3782	is cartesian over \(T' \to T\).
3783	
3784	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3785	[Pullback preserves finite presentation]
3786	  \label{lem:pullback_finite_presentation}
3787	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isFinitePresentation}
3788	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1 (read from
3789	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L418-L419):
3790	  % "Then as properness and flatness are preserved by base-change, and as
3791	  %  tensor-product is right exact, the pull-back of <F, q> ... is
3792	  %  well-defined"; cf. Stacks, Modules, "pullback of finitely presented
3793	  %  modules is finitely presented".
3794	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3795	  Let \(g : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) a finitely
3796	  presented sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (locally the cokernel of a map
3797	  of finite free modules).  Then \(g^* \mathcal{F}\) is a finitely presented
3798	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules.
3799	\end{lemma}
3800	\begin{proof}
3801	  % NOTE: the Lean proof is complete modulo the index-bookkeeping leaf
3802	  % `Modules.pullbackSlicePresentation_isFinite` (the transported presentation
3803	  % keeps its index types; the definitional reduction certifying this is
3804	  % recorded as a typed sorry for performance reasons).
3805	  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on whose members \(\mathcal{F}\)
3806	  admits presentations
3807	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus b_i} \to \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus a_i} \to
3808	  \mathcal{F}|_{U_i} \to 0\) with \(a_i, b_i\) finite.  The preimages
3809	  \(g^{-1}(U_i)\) cover \(Y\).  The pullback functor is a left adjoint, hence
3810	  right exact, and it sends \(\mathcal{O}_X\) to \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) and finite
3811	  free modules to finite free modules of the same rank; moreover restriction
3812	  to \(g^{-1}(U_i)\) of the pullback is the pullback of the restriction along
3813	  \(g^{-1}(U_i) \to U_i\).  Applying the restricted pullback to the chosen
3814	  presentation therefore yields a presentation
3815	  \(\mathcal{O}^{\oplus b_i} \to \mathcal{O}^{\oplus a_i} \to
3816	  (g^*\mathcal{F})|_{g^{-1}(U_i)} \to 0\) with the same finite index sets.
3817	\end{proof}
3818	
3819	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3820	[Flatness over the base is stable under base change]
3821	  \label{lem:coherent_flat_base_change}
3822	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat.of_isPullback}
3823	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
3824	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1 (read from
3825	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L418-L419);
3826	  % cf. Stacks 01U9 (flatness of morphisms is stable under base change).
3827	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Stacks 01U9.}
3828	  Let
3829	  \[
3830	    \begin{array}{ccc}
3831	      X' & \xrightarrow{\ g'\ } & X \\
3832	      \downarrow f' & & \downarrow f \\
3833	      S' & \xrightarrow{\ g\ } & S
3834	    \end{array}
3835	  \]
3836	  be a cartesian square of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf
3837	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is flat over \(S\) in the sense of
3838	  \cref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}.  Then \(g'^* \mathcal{F}\) is flat over
3839	  \(S'\).
3840	\end{lemma}
3841	\begin{proof}
3842	  For a quasi-coherent module, the affine-pair flatness predicate of
3843	  \cref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat} is equivalent to flatness of every stalk
3844	  \(\mathcal{F}_x\) over the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S, f(x)}\): a module
3845	  over a ring \(B\) with an action of \(A\) through \(A \to B\) is flat over
3846	  \(A\) if and only if all its localizations at primes of \(B\) are flat over
3847	  the corresponding localizations of \(A\).  The stalk of \(g'^*\mathcal{F}\)
3848	  at \(x' \in X'\) is
3849	  \(\mathcal{F}_{g'(x')} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, g'(x')}}
3850	  \mathcal{O}_{X', x'}\), and since the square is cartesian
3851	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X', x'}\) is a localization of
3852	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X, g'(x')} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{S, s}}
3853	  \mathcal{O}_{S', s'}\) (where \(s = f(g'(x'))\), \(s' = f'(x')\)).  Hence
3854	  \((g'^*\mathcal{F})_{x'}\) is a localization of
3855	  \(\mathcal{F}_{g'(x')} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{S,s}} \mathcal{O}_{S', s'}\);
3856	  flatness over \(\mathcal{O}_{S,s}\) base-changes to flatness over
3857	  \(\mathcal{O}_{S', s'}\) and is preserved by localization.
3858	\end{proof}
3859	
3860	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3861	[Proper support is stable under base change]
3862	  \label{lem:proper_support_base_change}
3863	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport.of_isPullback}
3864	  \uses{def:has_proper_support, def:modules_annihilator}
3865	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1 (read from
3866	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L418-L419);
3867	  % cf. Stacks 01W4 (properness is stable under base change) and 056H
3868	  % (support of the pullback of a finite-type module).
3869	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3870	  In the cartesian square of \cref{lem:coherent_flat_base_change}, let
3871	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a finitely presented sheaf of
3872	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose schematic support is proper over \(S\)
3873	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}).  Then the schematic support of
3874	  \(g'^* \mathcal{F}\) is proper over \(S'\).
3875	\end{lemma}
3876	\begin{proof}
3877	  A local section of \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) pulls back to a section
3878	  annihilating \(g'^* \mathcal{F}\), so the pullback ideal
3879	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F}) \cdot \mathcal{O}_{X'}\) is contained in
3880	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(g'^*\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}).
3881	  Consequently the schematic support of \(g'^* \mathcal{F}\) is a closed
3882	  subscheme of the base change
3883	  \(Z' := Z \times_X X' = Z \times_S S'\) of the schematic support
3884	  \(Z\) of \(\mathcal{F}\).  Since \(Z \to S\) is proper and properness is
3885	  stable under base change, \(Z' \to S'\) is proper; a closed immersion
3886	  followed by a proper morphism is proper, so the schematic support of
3887	  \(g'^* \mathcal{F}\) is proper over \(S'\).
3888	\end{proof}
3889	
3890	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3891	[The fibrewise Hilbert function is invariant under base change]
3892	  \label{lem:hilbert_fibre_base_change}
3893	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertFunction_quotBaseMap}
3894	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
3895	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
3896	  % Polynomials" (read from
3897	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L498):
3898	  % the decomposition Quot = \coprod_\Phi Quot^\Phi presupposes that the
3899	  % Hilbert polynomial of the fibres is invariant under base change.
3900	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3901	  Let \(\pi : X \to S\) be locally of finite type, \(L\) a sheaf of
3902	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, \(T' \to T\) a morphism of \(S\)-schemes, and
3903	  \(\mathcal{F}\) a finitely presented sheaf on \(X_T\) whose schematic
3904	  support is proper over \(T\).  For \(t' \in T'\) with image \(t \in T\)
3905	  and every \(m \in \mathbb{N}\),
3906	  \[
3907	    \dim_{\kappa(t')} \Gamma\bigl((X_{T'})_{t'},\,
3908	      (g'^*\mathcal{F})_{t'} \otimes L_{t'}^{\otimes m}\bigr)
3909	    \;=\;
3910	    \dim_{\kappa(t)} \Gamma\bigl((X_T)_t,\,
3911	      \mathcal{F}_t \otimes L_t^{\otimes m}\bigr),
3912	  \]
3913	  where \(g' : X_{T'} \to X_T\) is the induced morphism.  In particular the
3914	  graded Hilbert function of \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} is invariant under
3915	  base change of the parameter scheme.
3916	\end{lemma}
3917	\begin{proof}
3918	  Since \(X_{T'} = X_T \times_T T'\), the fibre of \(X_{T'} \to T'\) at
3919	  \(t'\) is the base change \((X_T)_t \times_{\operatorname{Spec} \kappa(t)}
3920	  \operatorname{Spec} \kappa(t')\) of the fibre at \(t\) along the residue
3921	  field extension \(\kappa(t) \to \kappa(t')\), and under this identification
3922	  \((g'^*\mathcal{F})_{t'} \otimes L_{t'}^{\otimes m}\) is the pullback of
3923	  \(\mathcal{F}_t \otimes L_t^{\otimes m}\).  Sections of a module
3924	  annihilated by the ideal of a closed subscheme are computed on that
3925	  subscheme, so both sides are computed on the support of the twisted
3926	  module, which is proper — in particular quasi-compact and separated — over
3927	  the respective residue field.  Global sections of a quasi-coherent module
3928	  on a qcqs scheme commute with flat base change of the (affine) base; a
3929	  field extension \(\kappa(t) \to \kappa(t')\) is flat, so
3930	  \(\Gamma\) of the base-changed module is
3931	  \(\Gamma\bigl((X_T)_t, \mathcal{F}_t \otimes L_t^{\otimes m}\bigr)
3932	  \otimes_{\kappa(t)} \kappa(t')\).  Finally
3933	  \(\dim_{\kappa(t')} (V \otimes_{\kappa(t)} \kappa(t')) =
3934	  \dim_{\kappa(t)} V\) for any \(\kappa(t)\)-vector space \(V\) (in the
3935	  infinite-dimensional case both dimensions are infinite, and both sides of
3936	  the displayed equality carry the same junk value).
3937	\end{proof}
3938	
3939	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
3940	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
3941	
3942	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
3943	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
3944	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
3945	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
3946	
3947	\begin{definition}\leanok
3948	[Grassmannian scheme]
3949	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
3950	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
3951	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
3952	  % NOTE (run 0010 T12): the Lean definition is now REAL
3953	  % (AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotFunctorDef.lean), encoding the displayed set of
3954	  % rank-d locally free quotients of V_T directly (`Scheme.LocallyFreeQuotient`,
3955	  % Setoid quotient, pullback action along \psi.left).  The definition does NOT
3956	  % factor through def:quot_functor: the locally-free encoding is the form of the
3957	  % universal property valid over an arbitrary base, and it is the form matched by
3958	  % the merged chart construction (`AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor`).  The
3959	  % identification with the Quot special case (final sentence below) holds in the
3960	  % locally noetherian regime and is future work, not a definitional dependency.
3961	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
3962	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
3963	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
3964	  % + L773-L850).
3965	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3966	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
3967	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
3968	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
3969	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
3970	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
3971	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
3972	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
3973	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
3974	  %  contravariant functor
3975	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
3976	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
3977	  %  set of all equivalence classes
3978	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
3979	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
3980	  %  [...]
3981	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
3982	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
3983	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
3984	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
3985	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
3986	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
3987	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
3988	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
3989	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
3990	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
3991	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
3992	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3993	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
3994	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
3995	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
3996	  \[
3997	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
3998	    \qquad
3999	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
4000	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
4001	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
4002	  \]
4003	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
4004	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Over a locally
4005	  noetherian base this functor agrees with the Quot functor
4006	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
4007	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\): a flat finitely presented module whose fibre
4008	  dimension is \(d\) at every point is locally free of rank \(d\).
4009	\end{definition}

40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
54	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
56	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
57	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
58	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
61	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
62	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
63	  passim.}
64	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
65	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
66	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
67	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
68	  \(A \to B\)).
69	\end{definition}
70	
71	\begin{remark}
72	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
73	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
74	  pulling back along the cartesian square
75	  \[
76	    \begin{tikzcd}
77	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
78	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
79	    \end{tikzcd}
80	  \]
81	  yields a coherent \(\OO_{X'}\)-module \(\F' := (g')^* \F\) which is \(S'\)-flat
82	  whenever \(\F\) is \(S\)-flat.  This is the routine cancellation of tensor-product
83	  associativity at the level of stalks. The converse fails in general.
84	\end{remark}
85	
86	\section{Generic flatness}
87	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
88	
89	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
90	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
91	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
92	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
93	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
94	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
95	non-zero element of the base ring.
96	
97	\subsection{The dévissage layer}
98	\label{sec:generic_freeness_devissage}
99

2540	\end{proof}
2541	
2542	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2543	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2544	
2545	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2546	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2547	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2548	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2549	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2550	
2551	\begin{definition}
2552	
2553	\leanok
2554	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2555	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2556	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2557	  % NOTE: The definition carries no \uses: the Lean construction is
2558	  % `IdealSheafData.ofIdeals (fun U => Module.annihilator Γ(X,U) Γ(F,U))`, which is well-defined
2559	  % unconditionally and does NOT depend on the localization bridges at definition time. The two
2560	  % facts lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map and lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen are needed
2561	  % only for the downstream CHARACTERIZATION (the reverse inclusion / equality on affine opens, the
2562	  % "annihilator forward direction"), which is not yet a blueprint block.
2563	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2564	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2565	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2566	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2567	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2568	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2569	  % Polynomials" (read from
2570	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2571	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2572	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2573	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2574	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2575	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2576	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2577	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2578	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2579	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2580	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2581	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2582	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2583	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2584	  \[
2585	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2586	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2587	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2588	  \]
2589	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2590	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2591	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2592	
2593	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2594	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2595	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2596	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2597	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2598	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2599	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two

3600	  \[
3601	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \;\hookrightarrow\; X
3602	  \]
3603	  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) into the ambient
3604	  scheme, namely the subscheme immersion of the annihilator ideal sheaf
3605	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}). It is a
3606	  quasi-compact preimmersion onto the support, and it is the morphism whose
3607	  composite with a base morphism \(f : X \to S\) defines proper support
3608	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.
3609	\end{definition}
3610	
3611	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
3612	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
3613	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
3614	  \mathlibok
3615	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
3616	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
3617	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
3618	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
3619	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
3620	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
3621	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
3622	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
3623	\end{lemma}
3624	
3625	\begin{definition}
3626	
3627	\leanok
3628	[Proper support over the base]
3629	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
3630	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
3631	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:schematic_support_immersion, lem:isProper_mathlib}
3632	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
3633	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
3634	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
3635	  % + L418-L419).
3636	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3637	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
3638	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
3639	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
3640	  %  [...]
3641	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
3642	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
3643	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3644	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
3645	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
3646	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
3647	  \[
3648	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
3649	  \]
3650	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
3651	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
3652	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot
3653	  functor's pullback action requires.
3654	\end{definition}

41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{def:fiber_hilbert_function, lem:hilbertPolynomial_unique}
62	  \source{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
63	  % ENCODING. Formalized sorry-free in
64	  % AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/HilbertPolynomial.lean as a TOTAL definition on
65	  % the graded Hilbert function hilbertFunction π L F s
66	  % (def:fiber_hilbert_function): if some polynomial Φ ∈ ℚ[λ] agrees with the
67	  % graded Hilbert function at all m > N for some N, then
68	  % hilbertPolynomial π L F s is such a Φ, produced by classical choice --
69	  % canonical because uniqueness holds UNCONDITIONALLY
70	  % (lem:hilbertPolynomial_unique) -- and otherwise the junk value 0. The
71	  % definition carries no properness/ampleness hypotheses; the geometric
72	  % regime (proper X_s, ample L_s, coherent F_s) enters only through the
73	  % existence statement thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule and its
74	  % IsRatHilb-conditional form lem:hilbertPoly_of_isRatHilb. The construction
75	  % uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic χ by Serre
76	  % vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no numerical
77	  % invariant is lost.
78	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
79	  % Polynomials" (read from
80	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
81	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
82	  III.5.2.}
83	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
84	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
85	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
86	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
87	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
88	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
89	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
90	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
91	  (\cref{def:fiber_hilbert_function})
92	  \[
93	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
94	  \]
95	  of the section module
96	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
97	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
98	  over the homogeneous section ring
99	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
100	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Uniqueness of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is
101	  unconditional (\cref{lem:hilbertPolynomial_unique}: two polynomials agreeing
102	  at all sufficiently large integers coincide); existence, for \(X_s\) proper
103	  over \(\kappa(s)\), \(L_s\) ample and \(\mathcal{F}_s\) coherent, is the
104	  content of \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
105	
106	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
107	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
108	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
109	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
110	  \[
111	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
112	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
113	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
114	  \]

455	/-! ## §3. Families of quotients and the Quot functor -/
456	
457	section QuotFunctor
458	
459	variable [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
460	
461	/-- A **family of quotients of `E` parametrised by `T`** with Hilbert
462	polynomial `Φ` (Nitsure §1; the unbundled datum whose equivalence classes
463	form the value of the Quot functor `def:quot_functor` at `T`):
464	
465	* a finitely presented sheaf of modules `F` on the relative product
466	  `X_T = X ×_S T` (the coherence encoding of the flattening-stratification
467	  input; over the locally noetherian regime finite presentation ⟺
468	  coherence), flat over `T` and with schematic support proper over `T`;
469	* an epimorphism `q : E_T ⟶ F` from the pullback of `E` along the first
470	  projection;
471	* at every point `t : T`, the graded Hilbert function
472	  `m ↦ dim_{κ(t)} Γ((X_T)_t, F_t ⊗ L_t^{⊗m})` eventually agrees with `Φ` —
473	  by `Scheme.hilbertPolynomial_eq_of_eventually` this says exactly that the
474	  Hilbert polynomial of `F|_{X_t}` relative to `L|_{X_t}`
475	  (`def:hilbert_polynomial`) *is* `Φ`, and it excludes the junk-value
476	  coincidence when no polynomial matches. -/
477	structure QuotFamily (π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType π] (L E : X.Modules)
478	    (Φ : Polynomial ℚ) (T : Over S) : Type (u + 1) where
479	  /-- The quotient sheaf on the relative product `X ×_S T`. -/
480	  F : (Limits.pullback π T.hom).Modules
481	  /-- `F` is finitely presented (coherent, in the locally noetherian regime). -/
482	  isFinitePresentation : F.IsFinitePresentation
483	  /-- `F` is flat over `T`. -/
484	  flat : CoherentSheafFlat (pullback.snd π T.hom) F
485	  /-- The schematic support of `F` is proper over `T`. -/
486	  properSupport : Modules.HasProperSupport (pullback.snd π T.hom) F
487	  /-- The quotient map from the pullback of `E`. -/
488	  q : (Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.fst π T.hom)).obj E ⟶ F
489	  /-- The quotient map is an epimorphism. -/
490	  epi : Epi q
491	  /-- At every `t : T`, the graded Hilbert function of the fibre of `F`
492	  twisted by `L` eventually agrees with `Φ`. -/
493	  hilb : ∀ t : (T.left : Scheme.{u}), ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ m : ℕ, N < m →
494	    Φ.eval (m : ℚ) = (hilbertFunction (pullback.snd π T.hom)
495	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.fst π T.hom)).obj L) F t m : ℚ)
496	
497	namespace QuotFamily
498	
499	variable {π : X ⟶ S} [LocallyOfFiniteType π] {L E : X.Modules} {Φ : Polynomial ℚ}
500	
501	/-- Two families of quotients are **equivalent** when an isomorphism of the
502	target sheaves commutes with the quotient maps — equivalently, when
503	`ker q = ker q'` (`def:quot_functor`; same convention as the merged absolute
504	Grassmannian `RankQuotient.Rel`). -/
505	def Rel {T : Over S} (x y : QuotFamily π L E Φ T) : Prop :=
506	  ∃ f : x.F ≅ y.F, x.q ≫ f.hom = y.q
507	
508	lemma rel_refl {T : Over S} (x : QuotFamily π L E Φ T) : x.Rel x :=
509	  ⟨Iso.refl _, Category.comp_id _⟩

92	    (G : (π.fiber s).Modules) :
93	    Module (S.residueField s) Γ(G, ⊤) :=
94	  Module.compHom Γ(G, ⊤) (π.fiberResidueMap s).hom
95	
96	/-- The **graded Hilbert function** of `F` at the fibre over `s : S`, relative
97	to the line bundle `L`:
98	
99	`m ↦ dim_{κ(s)} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`,
100	
101	the dimension over the residue field `κ(s)` of the global sections of the
102	`m`-th twist of the restriction `F_s` by `L_s` on the scheme-theoretic fibre
103	`X_s = π.fiber s`.  This is the graded Hilbert function of the section graded
104	module `M(X_s, F_s, L_s)` (`def:sectionGradedModule`); it uses only `H⁰`.
105	When the dimension is not finite, `Module.finrank` takes the junk value `0`.
106	Blueprint: the graded Hilbert function of `def:hilbert_polynomial`. -/

108	    (m : ℕ) : ℕ :=

293	[Graded Hilbert function along a fibre]
294	  \label{def:fiber_hilbert_function}
295	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertFunction}
296	  \uses{def:fiber_module_restriction, def:fiber_sections_module,
297	    def:sheafModuleTwist}
298	  \source{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
299	  % ENCODING. Scheme.hilbertFunction π L F s m is
300	  % Module.finrank (S.residueField s)
301	  % Γ(Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow (π.fiberModule s F) (π.fiberModule s L) m, ⊤)
302	  % with the κ(s)-module structure brought into scope by
303	  % π.fiberSectionsModule s (def:fiber_sections_module); the twist is
304	  % def:sheafModuleTwist of chapter Picard_SectionGradedRing applied on the
305	  % fibre. Module.finrank returns the junk value 0 when the dimension is not
306	  % finite, so the Lean function is total. Formalized sorry-free in
307	  % AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/HilbertPolynomial.lean.
308	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
309	  Let \(S\) be a locally noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a morphism
310	  locally of finite type, \(L\) a line bundle and \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of
311	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules on \(X\), and \(s \in S\). The \emph{graded
312	  Hilbert function of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
313	  function
314	  \[
315	    h_{\mathcal{F},s} : \mathbb{N} \longrightarrow \mathbb{N}, \qquad
316	    h_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) \;=\;
317	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
318	  \]
319	  where \(\mathcal{F}_s\) and \(L_s\) are the restrictions to the fibre
320	  \(X_s\) (\cref{def:fiber_module_restriction}), the twist
321	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) is the \(m\)-twist of
322	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\) by \(L_s\) on \(X_s\) (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}), and
323	  the dimension is taken with respect to the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector-space
324	  structure on sections over the fibre (\cref{def:fiber_sections_module}).
325	  This is exactly the graded Hilbert function of the section graded module
326	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); it uses
327	  only global sections \(H^0\).
328	\end{definition}

1398	
1399	/-- A coherent sheaf `𝓕` on `X` is **flat over `S`** for a morphism
1400	`f : X ⟶ S` if, for every affine open `U ⊆ S` and every affine open
1401	`V ⊆ f⁻¹U`, the `𝓞_X`-module of sections `Γ(𝓕, V)` is flat as a
1402	`Γ(S, U)`-module, where the `Γ(S, U)`-action is the restriction of
1403	scalars along the ring morphism `Γ(S, U) → Γ(X, V)` (the `appLE`
1404	morphism of `f`).
1405	
1406	This is the affine-local form of Stacks 00HB (a coherent sheaf is flat
1407	over the base if every stalk is flat over the corresponding base
1408	stalk); the affine-local form and stalk form are equivalent for
1409	*quasi-coherent* sheaves on a noetherian base, which is the regime of
1410	the flattening-stratification theorem.
1411

data.content:
---
name: horizon-commit
description: Record your own work in the workspace ledger with semantic commits via `horizon commit` — small, meaningful, math-first messages; safe under concurrent runs.
---

Commit your work yourself, semantically, as you finish coherent pieces — don't
leave one giant undifferentiated snapshot for the system to sweep up. Good
commits are how the dashboard's change view and a human reviewer understand what
each session actually did.

## How

Use the Horizon CLI (never raw `git` on the ledger — the workspace repo is
out-of-tree with excludes, a secret guard, and provenance trailers that a bare
`git` would bypass):

- `"$HORIZON_BIN" commit -m "<message>" <files…>` — commit the files you changed.
  Paths are relative to your current directory (your project dir) or absolute.
- `"$HORIZON_BIN" commit -m "<message>" --changed` — commit everything you
  changed in the project (excludes the shared Horizon state, which the system
  commits separately).

Provenance (run / session / role / task / projects) is stamped automatically —
do **not** pass it. The commit is attributed to your role.

## Messages — math first

Write the message the way you'd tell a mathematician what you did: the result
and the idea, in human-readable prose with LaTeX where it helps; mention Lean
declaration names / files only as supporting detail. Mirror the house style,
e.g. `Prove H^i(X,F)=0 for i>dim X via the Čech-to-derived comparison`. One
coherent step per commit; split unrelated work into separate commits.

## Concurrency — you are not alone

Other runs may be committing to the **same** ledger at the same time. That's
fine and intended (it helps everyone converge faster):

- Commits are a single shared branch — they serialise and never conflict as a
  git *merge*; if `horizon commit` hits contention it already retried, so trust

Functor laws derived once from the pseudofunctor coherence of the module pullback (unitality/associativity conjugated along base-change triangles, Modules.pullback_id_app_coherence / pullback_comp_app_coherence). Base-change well-definedness factored into named leaves: pullback of finitely presented is finitely presented (proved up to an index-bookkeeping leaf); flatness, proper support, and Hilbert-function invariance pinned as true typed sorries with blueprint nodes (sec:quot_base_change). Representability statements now quantify over the real functors; old typed-sorry stubs excised from QuotScheme.lean (3 sorries remain there, all FBC-gated)." data.content: Launching skill: blueprint-conventions

text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/Axel/LeanAlgebraicGeometry-Horizon/.claude/skills/blueprint-conventions

Blueprints are a LaTeX subset (the `leanblueprint` dialect) parsed into the DAG.
Write timeless mathematics — a standalone document, not a project journal.

## Environments

Each declaration is an amsthm-style environment; the recognized ones are
`theorem`, `lemma`, `proposition`, `corollary`, `definition`, `remark`,
`example`, `notation`, `convention`, and `proof`. A statement environment plus
its following `proof` environment form one node's content.

## Per-node macros

- `\label{id}` — a stable id (the DAG node key).
- `\lean{Name}` — the Lean declaration this node formalizes. Keep it 1-to-1: one
  blueprint node ↔ one Lean declaration, signatures matching.
- `\uses{a,b}` — dependencies on other nodes' labels; these drive the DAG edges.
  Add an edge instead of re-explaining a dependency in prose. Put `\uses` in the
  statement for definitional deps and in the `proof` for deps used only in the
  proof.
- `\source{slug:page-0001}` — reference anchors for UI popups and audit trails.
  Use page-level ids from `references/<slug>/tex/page-0001.tex` or an equivalent
  manifest entry. Multiple anchors may be comma-separated.
- `\leanok` — the Lean is written and checked. Honest only when its Lean is
  actually checked *and* its dependencies are too. On a `proof`, it means the
  proof is formalized; on a statement, the signature is formalized.
- `\mathlibok` — the declaration is already available in mathlib (no
  formalization needed; treat as a leaf).

## House style

- **Pure mathematics only.** No Lean tactics, typeclass/implementation notes, or
  semi-Lean pseudocode. No project history ("since iter N", "our failed route")
  and no conversational filler. If a Lean helper exists with no math counterpart,
  write its LaTeX statement so the 1-to-1 correspondence holds.
- **Complete proofs, not sketches.** A node's `proof` is a real, rigorous
  mathematical proof a reader could check — not a hand-wave or a TODO. The way to
  keep it short is to *split*, not to abbreviate: if a proof is long or a node
  grows past a short paragraph, break out the hard step as its own `\uses`-linked
  lemma (down to sentence-sized lemmas if needed) so each node stays small and
  its proof stays complete.
- **One declaration's worth per node.** Don't restate Lean source as prose.
- **Cite by source link, not quote comments.** Add `\source{...}` to the
  relevant node. Keep the retrieved/transcribed page text in `references/` (for
  example `references/<slug>/tex/page-0042.tex`) and let the UI expose it; do not
  paste verbatim source quotes into `% QUOTE` comments inside the blueprint.

text:
Review complete. All checks were read-only; no files were edited.

## Defects found

**File for all items unless noted:** `/home/Axel/LeanAlgebraicGeometry-Horizon/MainProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`

1. **lem:hilbert_fibre_base_change — missing quasi-coherence hypothesis on `L` (substantive gap).** The statement allows `L` to be an arbitrary sheaf of `O_X`-modules (matching the Lean pin, which takes `L : X.Modules`), but the proof's key step — "global sections of a *quasi-coherent* module on a qcqs scheme commute with flat base change" — applies only if `F_t ⊗ L_t^{⊗m}` is quasi-coherent, i.e. only if `L` is. The same holds for the silent pushforward/base-change compatibility (item 2). Note `def:fiber_hilbert_function` (line 294) already requires `L` a *line bundle*, so the lemma is also internally inconsistent with the definition it transports. Fix: add "`L` quasi-coherent" (or "a line bundle") to the lemma, and flag to Horizon that the Lean sorry `hilbertFunction_quotBaseMap` at arbitrary `L : X.Modules` may be unprovable as stated.

2. **lem:hilbert_fibre_base_change — silent step in the proof.** Between "both sides are computed on the support" and the flat-base-change application, the proof uses without statement that the pullback of the pushforward from the support equals the pushforward of the pullback (base change along the affine/closed immersion of the support, again needing quasi-coherence). Fix: make this compatibility explicit, ideally as its own `\uses`-linked lemma per the split rule. (The routing of `Γ` through the proper — hence qcqs — support fibre `Z_t` is otherwise *correct* and does handle non-quasi-compact total fibres; the junk-value remark is mathematically right once `Γ' = Γ ⊗_{κ(t)} κ(t')` is established, since a `κ(t)`-basis stays a `κ(t')`-basis, but "junk value" is a Lean-ism in rendered proof text.)

3. **lem:hilbert_fibre_base_change — wrong dependency target.** The statement/`\uses` cite `def:hilbert_polynomial`, but the object transported is the *graded Hilbert function*, i.e. `def:fiber_hilbert_function` (whose `\lean` pin is exactly `hilbertFunction`). Fix: point `\uses` and the "In particular" `\cref` at `def:fiber_hilbert_function`.

4. **lem:hilbert_fibre_base_change — signature mismatch with Lean.** Blueprint assumes "π : X → S locally of finite type"; the Lean `hilbertFunction_quotBaseMap` (QuotFunctorDef.lean:442) has no `LocallyOfFiniteType π` in scope (only `variable {S X : Scheme.{u}}` at line 251 applies). Fix: drop the hypothesis from the blueprint (it is unused — everything happens on the proper support) or add it to the Lean.

5. **lem:coherent_flat_base_change — proof mischaracterizes its `\uses` definition, and applies it out of scope.** The proof invokes "the affine-pair flatness predicate of def:coherent_sheaf_flat", but that def (Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex:50–69) has the *stalk* condition as its primary form, restricted to S noetherian, f finite type, F coherent — while the Lean `CoherentSheafFlat` (FlatteningStratification.lean:1416) is the unrestricted affine-pair predicate, and this lemma applies the predicate to merely quasi-coherent F in an arbitrary cartesian square. The 00HT-style claim itself ("M over B flat over A iff every M_q is flat over A_{q∩A}") *is* correctly stated, and the stalk computation/localization steps are all correct. Fix: restate `def:coherent_sheaf_flat` at the Lean's generality (affine-pair predicate, arbitrary morphism/module) and split the stalk-criterion equivalence for quasi-coherent modules into its own `\uses`-linked lemma consumed here.

6. **lem:proper_support_base_change — missing proof-level `\uses`.** The proof is mathematically correct (the annihilator inclusion does give a closed immersion of the support-scheme of `g'^*F` into `V(Ann(F)·O_{X'}) = Z ×_X X' = Z ×_S S'`, and proper ∘ closed-immersion is proper), but it needs: (i) `g'^*F` finitely presented for `Ann(g'^*F)`/its schematic support to be well-defined per `def:modules_annihilator` — add `\uses{lem:pullback_finite_presentation}` in the proof; (ii) `lem:isProper_mathlib` for base-change stability and the composition fact. Also `def:modules_annihilator` (line 2551) is scoped "over a locally noetherian base" while this lemma runs on arbitrary schemes — relax the def's parenthetical to "quasi-coherent of finite type" or record the hypothesis here.

7. **All four new lemmas — no `\source{}` anchors.** They carry only `% SOURCE:` quote comments plus `\textit{Source: ...}` prose; house rule is a structural `\source{...}` anchor and no `% QUOTE` blocks. Fix: add `\source{nitsure-hilbert-quot}` (slug already used by `def:hilbert_polynomial`, line 62) and drop the verbatim quote comments.

8. **def:grassmannian_scheme — unproven assertion embedded in a definition.** The reworded final sentence claims "a flat finitely presented module whose fibre dimension is d at every point is locally free of rank d" (true) and identifies the functor with the Quot special case via a bare `\cref{def:quot_functor}` outside the DAG. Fix: move the agreement claim into a `\uses`-linked remark/lemma with proof, or mark it explicitly as forward-referenced future work in rendered text. Minor: the def says "S noetherian" while the Lean `Grassmannian` (and `QuotFunctor`) require only `[IsLocallyNoetherian S]` / `[LocallyOfFiniteType π]` — same strictness mismatch in `def:quot_functor`'s statement.

## Verified fine

## Summary

T12 (`AJC.picrep`): the two headline **definitions** of the Quot-scheme chapter are now **real Lean constructions**. `Scheme.QuotFunctor` is the genuine Quot functor — value at `T` the Setoid quotient of families `(F, q : E_T ↠ F)` on `X ×_S T` (finitely presented, flat over `T`, proper schematic support, fibrewise Hilbert polynomial `Φ` via eventual-match), morphism action pullback along `id_X ×_S ψ` — and `Scheme.Grassmannian` is the rank-`d` locally-free-quotient functor, **fully axiom-clean**. The functor laws are derived once from Mathlib's pseudofunctor coherence for the module pullback, packaged as two reusable app-level lemmas. The four base-change facts behind well-definedness are factored into named lemmas (finite presentation proved up to an index-bookkeeping leaf; flatness, proper support, Hilbert invariance pinned as true typed sorries with new blueprint nodes carrying complete proofs). Both representability theorems are now stated against the real functors. Full `lake build` green (8648 jobs, twice). A fresh-context blueprint review caught a genuine truth condition — Hilbert-fibre invariance can fail for non-quasi-coherent `L` — which I fixed by threading `[L.IsQuasicoherent]` through the lemma, `QuotFunctor`, and `QuotScheme` before it could become a false pinned statement.

## Progress
- QuotFunctorDef.lean (new, 840 lines): real `QuotFunctor` + `Grassmannian` (`Grassmannian` axiom-clean); coherence lemmas `pullback_id/comp_app_coherence(_inv)` proved; `quotBaseMap` calculus + cartesian square proved; 6 sorries total.

## Issues
